title: Erdos Problem #879
background:
# EP-879

## Problem Statement
The problem asks about maximizing total sum for special pairwise-coprime admissible subsets of $\{1,\dots,n\}$ (denoted by a function like $G(n)$) and comparing this with a benchmark quantity $H(n)$, including asymptotic gap behavior and structural questions about prime-factor richness.

## Literature
- Erdos-van Lint established strong bounds of the form
  $$H(n)-n^{3/2-o(1)}<G(n)<H(n),$$
  and divergence of $(H(n)-G(n))/n$.
- Stronger near-linear error terms are known only conditionally in the background notes.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built an exact finite proxy optimization:
  - maximize sum of pairwise-coprime subsets of $[1..n]$ for moderate $n$ exactly,
  - compare against a simple greedy lower construction.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Exact optimizer and greedy lower are often close, but not always equal.
- In tested $n=16,18,20,22,24$, exact maxima are modestly above greedy in several cases.
- This supports that local optimization landscape is nontrivial even at small scales.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone exact small-$n$ proxy optimization and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #881
background:
# EP-881

## Problem Statement
Let $A\subset\mathbb N$ be a minimal additive basis of order $k$ (removing any infinite subset destroys order-$k$ basis property). Must there exist an infinite $B\subset A$ such that $A\setminus B$ is a basis of order $k+1$?

## Literature
- The repository source had malformed metadata around this entry, but the mathematical statement above is recoverable from the normalized catalog text.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built a finite-horizon analog for $k=2\to3$:
  - search for finite minimal 2-bases on intervals $[L,U]$,
  - test whether deleting a small subset yields a 3-basis on the same interval.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- In tested regimes, finite minimal 2-bases were found repeatedly.
- In a substantial fraction of found instances, removing a small subset (often size 1) produced a finite-horizon 3-basis.
- This is suggestive finite behavior only; it does not resolve the infinite theorem.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone finite $k=2\to3$ transition probe and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #883
background:
# EP-883

## Problem Statement
At the threshold
$$|A|>\left\lfloor\frac n2\right\rfloor+\left\lfloor\frac n3\right\rfloor-\left\lfloor\frac n6\right\rfloor,$$
questions ask whether a graph $G(A)$ must contain:
1. all odd cycles up to about $n/3+1$;
2. for fixed $\ell$, a complete tripartite $(1,\ell,\ell)$ subgraph for large $n$.

## Literature
- The second question is known positive in strong form (with growing $\ell$), hence true for each fixed $\ell$ eventually.
- For odd cycles, known results in notes give a weaker linear-range guarantee, not yet the full stated range.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built finite proxy in an inferred graph model:
  - vertices $1..n$,
  - edges by distance set membership $|u-v|\in A$.
- At threshold-size random sets $A$, tested presence of all odd cycle lengths in target range.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- In tested instances ($n=60,90,120$), all targeted odd cycle lengths appeared in all trials.
- This supports the statement direction in finite random model, but depends on inferred graph interpretation and is not a proof.

## Status
- First subquestion: open in full stated strength.
- Second subquestion: treated as resolved in literature notes.
- Added standalone finite odd-cycle coverage probe and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #884
background:
# EP-884

## Problem Statement
For divisors $d_1<\cdots<d_t$ of $n$, is it true that
$$\sum_{1\le i<j\le t}\frac1{d_j-d_i}\ll 1+\sum_{1\le i<t}\frac1{d_{i+1}-d_i},$$
with an absolute implied constant?

## Literature
- The local dataset had malformed metadata around this entry, but the inequality statement itself is available.
- The core question is whether a uniform ratio bound holds over all $n$.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Performed direct finite scan up to $n\le 200000$.
- Computed ratio
  $$R(n)=\frac{\sum_{i<j}1/(d_j-d_i)}{\sum_i 1/(d_{i+1}-d_i)}$$
for each $n$ with at least 3 divisors.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Maximum observed ratio in this range:
  $$R(n)\approx 3.56587\quad\text{at }n=166320.$$
- Running maxima stayed in a small-constant range over checkpoints.
- Finite evidence is consistent with the conjectured bounded-ratio phenomenon.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone finite ratio scan and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #885
background:
# EP-885

## Problem Statement
For $D(n)=\{|a-b|: n=ab\}$, is it true that for every $k\ge1$ there exist
$$N_1<\cdots<N_k\quad\text{with}\quad\left|\bigcap_{i=1}^k D(N_i)\right|\ge k?$$

## Literature
- Known solved cases in notes: $k=2,3,4$.
- General $k$ remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Ran deeper finite search up to $N=120000$.
- Built support sets of differences and searched for $k$-tuples of differences with intersection size at least $k$ via bounded-depth intersection DFS.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Found explicit witnesses for $k=2,3$ in the searched range.
- Did not find witnesses for $k=4,5,6$ under this bounded candidate strategy.
- Non-findings are inconclusive and compatible with known positive theorem for $k=4$.

## Status
- Open in full generality.
- Added standalone deeper finite intersection search and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #887
background:
# EP-887

## Problem Statement
Is there an absolute constant $K$ such that for every fixed $C>0$ and all sufficiently large $n$, the interval
$$(n^{1/2},\, n^{1/2}+C n^{1/4})$$
contains at most $K$ divisors of $n$?

## Literature
- Erdos-Rosenfeld established existence of infinitely many $n$ with 4 divisors in the $C=1$ interval, and asked whether 4 is optimal there.
- Global uniform boundedness in $C$ remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Ran a deeper finite scan up to $n\le 3\cdot 10^6$.
- For each $C\in\{1,2,3,4,6\}$, counted divisors in the target interval and tracked maxima.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Max counts found in range:
  - $C=1$: 1,
  - $C=2$: 4,
  - $C=3$: 5,
  - $C=4$: 6,
  - $C=6$: 8.
- Finite evidence is consistent with boundedness for fixed $C$, but no universal constant conclusion follows.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone deeper divisor-near-sqrt scan and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #888
background:
# EP-888

## Problem Statement
Maximize $|A|\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}$ subject to:
for $a\le b\le c\le d$ in $A$, if $abcd$ is a square then necessarily $ad=bc$.

## Literature
- Known progress in notes:
  - upper bound $|A|=o(n)$,
  - lower bounds from primes and semiprimes, including
    $$|A|\gg \frac{n\log\log n}{\log n}.$$
- Sharp order of growth remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Computed exact optima for small $n$ by branch-and-bound search.
- Computed greedy finite profiles for larger $n$ across multiple trials.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Exact small-$n$ best sizes for $n=22,24,26,28$ are $14,15,16,16$.
- Greedy best sizes for $n\in\{80,100,120,150,180\}$ remain a substantial fraction of $n$ in this finite range.
- Finite data shows dense feasible families exist locally, but asymptotic behavior is still unresolved.

## Status
- Open.

title: Erdos Problem #889
background:
# EP-889

## Problem Statement
For
$$v(n,k)=\#\{p\mid n+k:\ p\nmid n+i\ (0\le i<k)\},$$
equivalently number of prime factors of $n+k$ exceeding $k$, define
$$v_0(n)=\max_{k\ge0} v(n,k).$$
Is $v_0(n)\to\infty$ as $n\to\infty$?

## Literature
- Erdos-Selfridge conjectured stronger variants $v_\ell(n)\to\infty$ for fixed $\ell$.
- Even basic lower bounds are hard in the classical discussion.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Ran deeper bounded-$k$ finite scan with
  $$n\le 5000,\quad 0\le k\le 15000.$$
- Computed exact bounded proxy
  $$v_0^{(K)}(n)=\max_{0\le k\le K} v(n,k).$$

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Maximum observed bounded value in this range is 5.
- Prefix minima remain 1 across all checked prefixes up to 5000.
- Average bounded values increase slowly with prefix size.
- Finite bounded scans do not resolve the unbounded-$k$ asymptotic conjecture.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone deeper bounded-$k$ profile and normalized data integration.

# EP-890 deeper attempt with scans

## Statement
For fixed `k`, study
`S_k(n)=sum_{i=0}^{k-1} omega(n+i)`
and ask:
1. whether `liminf S_k(n) <= k+pi(k)`,
2. whether `limsup S_k(n) * loglog n / log n = 1`.

## Computation in this attempt
I scanned `S_k(n)` for `k<=12` up to `n<=2e6`, and for `k<=6` up to `n<=1e7`.

This is finite evidence only; it does not prove/disprove the asymptotic claims.

title: Erdos Problem #891

# EP-891

## Problem Statement
Let $2=p_1<p_2<\cdots$ be primes and fix $k\ge2$. Must every sufficiently large interval of length
$$p_1p_2\cdots p_k$$
contain an integer with more than $k$ prime factors?

## Literature
- A nearby positive variant is known (with length $p_1\cdots p_{k-1}p_{k+1}$).
- The exact stated length remains open, including classical interest for $k=2$.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Performed finite failure-window scan up to $X=300000$ for $k=2,3$.
- Checked both exact length and nearby variant length.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- For $k=2$:
  - exact length 6 has finite failures (at the start),
  - variant length 10 has no failures in scanned range.

title: Erdos Problem #892
background:
# EP-892

## Problem Statement
Characterize growth sequences $b_n$ for which there exists a primitive sequence $a_n$ with
$$a_n \ll b_n.$$

## Literature
- Classical necessary conditions are known (from Erdos and Erdos-Sarkozy-Szemeredi work), but a full characterization remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built finite greedy feasibility probe:
  - for model growth laws $b_n$,
  - test whether one can greedily construct a primitive sequence with $a_n\le C b_n$ for all $n\le M$.
- Used multiple $C$ values and models (`linear`, `nlogn`, `n_log2`, `quadratic`, `powers2`).

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Linear-scale $b_n$ needs larger constant before long finite success appears.
- Faster growth models (`n log n`, `n log^2 n`, quadratic) support full finite prefix construction even at small constants in this probe.
- Very sparse exponential-step model (`powers2`) fails early for moderate constants.
- These are heuristic finite diagnostics, not a proof of necessary/sufficient criteria.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone finite greedy feasibility probe and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #893
background:
# EP-893

## Problem Statement
Let
$$
f(n)=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\tau(2^j-1),
$$
where $\tau$ is the divisor-counting function. Study the behavior of
$$
\frac{f(2n)}{f(n)}.
$$
In particular, determine whether this ratio has finite limiting behavior.

## Literature
- Recent work (Kovac--Luca, 2025; cited in prior project notes) indicates non-finite limiting behavior, with unbounded limsup in the relevant formulation.
- The problem page is still tracked as open for full sharp asymptotic characterization.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Ran exact finite-prefix computation using explicit factorization-by-trial-division for $\tau(2^k-1)$ up to $k\le 48$.
- Computed exact prefix sums $f(n)$ and ratio profile $f(2n)/f(n)$ for all $2\le n\le 24$.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- In tested range, ratio spans from $3$ up to about $8.08148$.
- Peak in this run appears at $n=18$.
- The finite data is consistent with highly non-stationary behavior and with an unbounded-limsup heuristic.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deeper standalone ratio-profile computation in normalized single-file form.

title: Erdos Problem #896
background:
# EP-896

## Problem Statement
For $A,B\subseteq\{1,\dots,N\}$, let $F(A,B)$ be the number of integers $m$ that have exactly one representation
$$
m=ab,\qquad a\in A,\ b\in B.
$$
Determine the maximal possible value of $F(A,B)$ as a function of $N$.

## Literature
- Known two-sided bounds (as summarized in existing project notes) are of shape
$$
(1+o(1))\frac{N^2}{\log N}\ \le\ \max_{A,B}F(A,B)
$$
and an upper bound of type
$$
\max_{A,B}F(A,B)\ll \frac{N^2}{(\log N)^\delta(\log\log N)^{3/2}}
$$
for explicit $\delta>0$.
- Exact leading term and sharp logarithmic exponent remain open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Exact exhaustive optimization for small $N\in\{6,7,8,9,10\}$ by checking all pairs $(A,B)$.
- Deep constructive scans for larger $N\in\{1000,2000,4000,8000,12000\}$ using two families:
  - $A=[1,\lfloor N/2\rfloor]$, $B=$ primes in $(N/2,N]$.
  - $A=[1,N]$, $B=$ primes in $(N/2,N]$.
- Recorded unique-product counts, collision bins, and normalized ratio against $N^2/\log N$.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- For $A=[1,\lfloor N/2\rfloor]$, $B$ as above, collision bins are exactly $0$ in all tested deep cases, giving fully unique products in this construction.
- At $N=12000$ this construction gives $F=3{,}930{,}000$ and
$$
\frac{F}{N^2/\log N}\approx 0.25634.
$$
- Using full $A=[1,N]$ increases raw count but introduces collisions; at $N=12000$:
  - unique count $7{,}431{,}630$,
  - collision bins $214{,}185$,
  - normalized ratio $\approx 0.48474$.
- Small-$N$ exact maxima (up to $N=10$) provide calibration examples but are too small to determine asymptotic constants.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone deep exact+constructive computation in normalized single-file form.

title: Erdos Problem #901
background:
# EP-901

## Problem Statement
Let $m(n)$ denote the minimum number of edges in an $n$-uniform hypergraph that is not 2-colorable. Determine the correct growth/order of $m(n)$.

## Literature
- Classical lower bound: $m(n)\ge 2^{n-1}$ (probabilistic coloring argument).
- Known upper bounds are exponential in $n$ up to polynomial/exponential-factor refinements; exact asymptotic remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built a deeper multirestart greedy constructor for non-2-colorable $n$-uniform hypergraphs.
- For each $n\in\{4,5,6,7\}$, scanned several vertex counts $v$ and ran many randomized restarts with large candidate-edge sampling.
- Verified non-2-colorability by explicit elimination of all 2-colorings when a witness is found.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Best witnesses found in this run:
  - $n=4$: $28$ edges (at $v=9$), improving earlier in-repo batch witness $30$.
  - $n=5$: $67$ edges (at $v=11$), improving earlier in-repo batch witness $92$.
  - $n=6$: $321$ edges (at $v=13$), slightly improving earlier in-repo batch witness $327$.
- For $n=7$, this run did not find a full witness under the current search budget (best remaining colorings still positive), so deeper search is still needed there.
- Ratios against $2^n$ in this run: about $1.75$ ($n=4$), $2.09$ ($n=5$), $5.02$ ($n=6$).

## Status
- Open.
- Added deeper standalone constructive computation with improved finite witnesses for $n=4,5,6$.

title: Erdos Problem #906
background:
# EP-906

## Problem Statement
Does there exist a nonzero entire function $f$ such that for every infinite increasing sequence
$$
n_1<n_2<\cdots,
$$
the set
$$
\{z\in\mathbb C: f^{(n_k)}(z)=0\ \text{for some }k\}
$$
is dense in $\mathbb C$?

## Literature
- Existing in-repo commentary indicates the as-written formulation is likely too weak/trivial and that intended variants usually impose extra constraints (e.g. transcendental/non-polynomial behavior).

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Direct witness for the as-written existential statement:
  - choose $f(z)=1$ (nonzero entire), then for every $m\ge 1$, $f^{(m)}\equiv 0$.
  - hence for any infinite increasing sequence $(n_k)$ of positive integers, the derivative-zero set is all of $\mathbb C$.
- Added standalone script/data sanity check over multiple derivative-order sequences and a finite complex grid.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Finite checks are consistent with the exact argument: all tested derivative orders evaluate to zero identically for $f(z)=1$.

## Status
- As written: solved trivially (positive existential answer).
- Intended stronger variant with additional constraints remains separate/open.

title: Erdos Problem #911
background:
# EP-911

## Problem Statement
Let $\hat{R}(G)$ denote the size Ramsey number of $G$, i.e. the minimum number of edges
$m$ such that there exists a graph $H$ with $m$ edges and
$H \to (G,G)$ (every red/blue edge-coloring of $H$ has a monochromatic copy of $G$).

Is there a function $f$ with
\[
\frac{f(x)}{x}\to\infty \quad (x\to\infty)
\]
such that for all sufficiently large $C$, whenever $G$ has $n$ vertices and
$e(G)\ge Cn$, one has
\[
\hat{R}(G) > f(C)\,e(G)?
\]

## Literature
- The local dataset entry for EP-911 is malformed, but the intended statement above is recoverable from the readable prefix.
- For cliques, the size-Ramsey identity
\[
\hat{R}(K_t)=\binom{R(t,t)}{2}
\]
is classical (often attributed to Chvátal).
- Classical Ramsey bounds used computationally here:
\[
R(t,t) > 2^{t/2}
\]
(Erdős probabilistic lower bound), and
\[
R(t,t)\le \binom{2t-2}{t-1}
\]
(Erdős-Szekeres upper bound).

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone family computation (`scripts/ep911.mjs`) on dense graphs $G=K_t$:
  - computes $C=(t-1)/2$ and $e=\binom t2$;
  - converts Ramsey bounds into explicit lower/upper bounds on
    $\hat{R}(K_t)/e$ via $\hat{R}(K_t)=\binom{R(t,t)}2$;
  - tracks growth of $\bigl(\hat{R}(K_t)/e\bigr)/C$.

## Computation-Guided Observations
Deep run (`DEPTH=5`, `TMAX=80`) finds rapid growth in the clique family:
- already for moderate $t$, lower bounds force $\hat{R}(K_t)/e$ to dominate $C$ strongly;
- at $t=80$ ($C=39.5$), the lower-bound ratio satisfies
\[
\frac{\hat{R}(K_t)}{e} \gtrsim 1.9\times 10^{20},
\qquad
\frac{\hat{R}(K_t)/e}{C}\gtrsim 4.8\times10^{18}.
\]

This supports a strong positive trend on one dense family but does not resolve the
full EP-911 quantifier over all dense graphs.

## Status
- Statement repaired and normalized.
- Added explicit family-level deep computation; full problem remains open.

title: Erdos Problem #912
background:
# EP-912

## Problem Statement
If
$$
n!=\prod_i p_i^{k_i}
$$
is the prime factorization of $n!$, let $h(n)$ be the number of distinct exponent values among the $k_i$.
Is there a constant $c>0$ such that
$$
h(n)\sim c\left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right)^{1/2}?
$$

## Literature
- Erdos--Selfridge proved the order of growth
$$
h(n)\asymp \left(\frac{n}{\log n}\right)^{1/2}.
$$
- The exact leading constant $c$ remains unresolved in standard references/notes, with heuristic predictions discussed in background commentary.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Implemented exact incremental computation of $h(n)$ from $n=2$ up to $N=5\cdot 10^7$.
- Maintained exact prime-exponent profile of $n!$ and tracked the number of distinct exponent values at sampled checkpoints.
- Computed normalized ratio
$$
\frac{h(n)}{\sqrt{n/\log n}}
$$
to estimate finite-range constant behavior.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Final sample at $n=50{,}000{,}000$ gives
$$
\frac{h(n)}{\sqrt{n/\log n}}\approx 2.6485.
$$
- Over tail samples in this run, the ratio stays in a narrow band about $2.639$ to $2.666$, with mean about $2.6519$.
- Finite evidence strongly supports the known $\sqrt{n/\log n}$ scale and suggests stable constant-like behavior in this range.

## Status
- Order of growth: established in literature.
- Exact asymptotic constant: still open.
- Added deep standalone exact computation up to $N=5\times10^7$.

title: Erdos Problem #913
background:
# EP-913

## Problem Statement
Are there infinitely many $n$ such that if
$$
n(n+1)=\prod_i p_i^{k_i},
$$
then all exponents $k_i$ are distinct?

## Literature
- Existing background notes mention a conditional route via prime-pattern heuristics (e.g. constructions yielding exponent pattern $\{1,2,3\}$ under additional primality assumptions), but no unconditional infinitude proof is known here.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Performed deep finite scan up to $N=5\cdot 10^7$.
- For each $n\le N$, factored both $n$ and $n+1$ exactly and checked whether exponent multiset in $n(n+1)$ has pairwise distinct entries.
- Tracked witness counts, first witnesses, and checkpoint densities.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Found $1149$ witness values up to $5\times10^7$.
- Empirical witness density up to this range is about
$$
2.298\times 10^{-5}.
$$
- Early witnesses include many structured patterns (e.g. exponent sets $\{1,2,3\}$ appearing repeatedly).
- Finite data supports existence of many witnesses but does not decide infinitude.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deep standalone exact finite scan and normalized data integration.

title: Erdos Problem #917
background:
# EP-917

## Problem Statement
Let $k\ge 4$ and let $f_k(n)$ be the maximum number of edges in an $n$-vertex $k$-critical graph.
Main questions include whether $f_k(n)\gg_k n^2$ and, for $k=6$, whether $f_6(n)\sim n^2/4$.

## Literature
- Classical constructions (Dirac and later refinements) give dense $k$-critical lower bounds.
- For $k=6$, the join-of-two-odd-cycles family yields edge counts matching Dirac's formula on infinitely many $n$.
- Full asymptotic characterization remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built standalone exact colorability checker and applied it to Dirac-family graphs
$$
G_t=C_{2t+1}+C_{2t+1}
$$
for $t=2,\dots,12$.
- For each $t$, verified:
  - $G_t$ is not 5-colorable but is 6-colorable,
  - after deleting any single edge, the graph becomes 5-colorable (full edge-by-edge check).

## Computation-Guided Observations
- All tested instances are fully 6-critical in exact checks.
- Edge counts exactly match
$$
|E(G_t)|=4t^2+8t+3,
$$
with density decreasing toward $1/4$ as $t$ grows.
- At $t=12$ ($n=50$), we get $|E|=675$ and edge density $0.27$, consistent with approach to the $n^2/4$ scale from above.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deeper standalone exact finite verification for the core Dirac construction family.

title: Erdos Problem #918
background:
# EP-918

## Problem Statement
Is there a graph with $\aleph_2$ vertices and chromatic number $\aleph_2$ such that every subgraph on $\aleph_1$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq\aleph_0$?
Is there a graph with $\aleph_{\omega+1}$ vertices and chromatic number $\aleph_1$ such that every subgraph on $\aleph_\omega$ vertices has chromatic number $\leq\aleph_0$?

## Literature
- Erdos--Hajnal proved strong precursor results: for each finite $k$, there is a graph of chromatic number $\aleph_1$ whose subgraphs on fewer than $\aleph_k$ vertices are countably colorable.
- The stronger cardinal-jump formulations above remain open in standard references used in this repo.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Used finite proxy searches for global/local chromatic separation:
  - look for finite graphs that are globally not $k$-colorable,
  - while all medium-size induced subgraphs are $\ell$-colorable.
- Added one exhaustive regime (`n=18`, `m=9`) checking all $\binom{18}{9}$ subgraphs per trial, and one larger random-sampling regime (`n=28`, `m=16`).

## Computation-Guided Observations
- No finite proxy witnesses were found in these deep runs.
- In particular, the exhaustive proxy on `n=18,m=9` found essentially no graphs forcing global chromatic jump beyond 4 under the tested densities, and none satisfying the full separation condition.
- This negative finite signal aligns with the intuition that strong global/local chromatic separation is delicate and structurally constrained.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone deep finite-proxy search (including exhaustive local-subgraph checks in a calibrated regime).

title: Erdos Problem #919
background:
# EP-919

## Problem Statement
Is there a graph $G$ with vertex set $\omega_2^2$ and chromatic number $\aleph_2$ such that every subgraph whose vertices have lesser type has chromatic number $\leq\aleph_0$?
What if instead we ask for $G$ to have chromatic number $\aleph_1$?

## Literature
- Erdos--Hajnal gave the classical $\omega_1^2$-type lexicographic construction with a strong global/local separation pattern.
- Related constructions on $\omega_2^2$ with weaker local bound ($\leq\aleph_1$) are known.
- The stronger local collapse in this problem remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built finite analogue of the lex-square construction on $[m]\times[m]$ with lexicographic order and edge rule
$$
(a_1,b_1)\sim(a_2,b_2)\iff a_1<a_2\ \text{and}\ b_1<b_2.
$$
- Studied chromatic number of initial segments (finite proxy for “lesser type” substructures).
- Derived explicit prefix formula and verified it exactly by coloring computations for $m=4,\dots,10$.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Exact checks for all tested prefixes matched the formula
$$
\chi(\text{prefix of size }t)=q+\mathbf 1_{r\ge q+1},
$$
where $t=(q)m+r$ in row-wise lex decomposition.
- In particular, in this finite model:
  - full graph has chromatic number $m$,
  - every proper initial segment has chromatic number at most $m-1$.
- Large-scale profile up to $m=400$ shows the largest proper initial segment has ratio $\chi/m=399/400$.

## Status
- Open (infinite-cardinal statement).
- Added standalone finite analogue computation with exact small-case validation and large-scale prefix profiles.

title: Erdos Problem #920
background:
# EP-920

## Problem Statement
Let $f_k(n)$ be the maximum possible chromatic number of a $K_k$-free graph on $n$ vertices.
For $k\ge 4$, is it true that
$$
f_k(n) \gg \frac{n^{1-1/(k-1)}}{(\log n)^{c_k}}
$$
for some constant $c_k>0$?

## Literature
- Classical upper bounds (Graver--Yackel type) and modern Ramsey improvements give nontrivial bounds.
- For $k=4$, known results imply lower bounds of type
$$
f_4(n) \gg \frac{n^{2/3}}{(\log n)^{4/3}}
$$
from current $R(4,t)$ estimates, but the target form remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep finite study centered on $k=4$:
  - deeper Mycielski-chain verification (K4-free checks and chromatic profile),
  - randomized search over maximal $K_4$-free graphs with exact DSATUR chromatic computation.
- Ran a 10-seed ensemble; each seed performs exact-coloring search for $n\in\{20,24,28,32,36\}$.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Total multi-seed compute time in this run: about $13.7$ seconds.
- Ensemble best values found:
  - $n=20$: $\chi=5$,
  - $n=24$: $\chi=6$,
  - $n=28,32,36$: best remained $\chi=6$.
- The normalized quantity $\chi/n^{2/3}$ decreases across tested $n$, consistent with finite constructions still far from proving sharp asymptotic exponents.
- Mycielski rows remain robust finite lower-bound witnesses and stay $K_4$-free in verified range.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deep standalone multi-seed computation and normalized per-problem integration.

title: Erdos Problem #928
background:
# EP-928

## Problem Statement
Let $\alpha,\beta\in(0,1)$ and let $P(n)$ be the largest prime factor of $n$.
Does the natural density of integers $n$ such that
$$
P(n)<n^{\alpha},\qquad P(n+1)<(n+1)^{\beta}
$$
exist?

## Literature
- Dickman gives marginal smooth-number density via $\rho(1/\alpha)$.
- Logarithmic-density versions and conditional natural-density results are known in parts of the literature.
- Full unconditional natural density in this two-variable setting remains open in general.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep empirical scan with:
  - sieve-based exact largest-prime-factor table up to $N=30{,}000{,}000$,
  - dense $(\alpha,\beta)$ grid (81 pairs),
  - checkpoint convergence at $N=5,10,20,30$ million.
- Recorded joint density, product of marginals, covariance surrogate $(\text{joint}-\text{product})$, and ratio joint/product.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Runtime for this deeper run: about $6.2$ seconds.
- Persistent mild negative correlation across tested pairs: joint density stays below product density.
- Example at $N=30{,}000{,}000$:
  - $(\alpha,\beta)=(0.5,0.5)$: joint/product $\approx 0.9263$.
  - $(0.7,0.7)$: joint/product $\approx 0.9851$.
  - $(0.8,0.8)$: joint/product $\approx 0.9903$.
- As thresholds become less restrictive, ratio approaches $1$ from below, consistent with near-independence at higher smoothness exponents.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deeper standalone multi-parameter empirical density profile with checkpoint tracking.

title: Erdos Problem #929
background:
# EP-929

## Problem Statement
Let $k\ge 2$ and let $S(k)$ be the minimal $x$ such that there is a positive-density set of $n$ for which
$$
n+1,n+2,\dots,n+k
$$
are all divisible only by primes $\le x$.
Estimate $S(k)$, in particular whether $S(k)\ge k^{1-o(1)}$.

## Literature
- Classical sieve lower bounds and modern prime-gap arguments give nontrivial lower/upper constraints.
- A substantial asymptotic gap remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep finite-density proxy using sliding-window maxima of largest prime factors.
- Parameters in this run:
  - $N=20{,}000{,}000$ start points,
  - $k\in\{6,8,10,12,16,20,24,28,32,40,48,56,64\}$,
  - threshold scan up to $x=120$,
  - multiple density thresholds $\varepsilon\in\{10^{-5},5\cdot 10^{-5},10^{-4},2\cdot10^{-4}\}$.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Finite minimal $x$ with any occurrence increases roughly linearly with $k$ in tested range:
  - e.g. $x_{\mathrm{any}}(16)=17$, $x_{\mathrm{any}}(32)=31$, $x_{\mathrm{any}}(64)=61$.
- In this range and at this $N$, no tested pair reached density thresholds $10^{-4}$ or $2\cdot10^{-4}$.
- Signal is consistent with nontrivial growth of effective smoothness threshold as block length grows.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deeper standalone large-$N$ sliding-window profile for finite-density proxies of $S(k)$.

title: Erdos Problem #930

### Source: ep930_partial.md

# EP-930 partial

## Statement
For each `r`, ask if there is `k` so that for any `r` disjoint intervals
`I_1,...,I_r` of consecutive integers, each of length at least `k`,
the product `prod_i prod_{m in I_i} m` is not a perfect power.

## Attempt in this batch
I ran exact prime-exponent tests for equal-length disjoint intervals.
For each `k <= 12`, I searched counterexamples inside a finite window.

Data file:
- `data/ep930_interval_product_power_scan.json`

## Result
- `r=2`: equal-length counterexamples found for `k=1,2,3`.
- `r=3`: equal-length counterexamples found for `k=1,2,3,4,5`.
- In this scan (`N<=220`), no equal-length counterexample was found for
  `r=2` with `k>=4`, or `r=3` with `k>=6`.

## New variable-length search (r=2)
I then searched disjoint intervals with lengths in `[k, k+4]` (not necessarily
equal), exact exponent-gcd test, up to `N<=1200`.

Data / script:
- `scripts/ep930_variable_interval_counterexample_search.mjs`
- `data/ep930_variable_interval_counterexample_search.json`

### New finding
- For `r=2`, `k=4` already has a counterexample:
  - `I1=[8,14]` (length `7`)
  - `I2=[63,66]` (length `4`)
  - Product factorization:
    `2^14 * 3^6 * 5^2 * 7^2 * 11^2 * 13^2` (a perfect square).

### Additional finite evidence
- In this search window, no `r=2` counterexample was found for
  `k=5,6,7,8,9,10,11,12` under `N<=1200` and length range `[k, k+4]`.

## Hard point
All results remain finite-window. The global statement quantifies over all
large lengths and all disjoint interval placements, so this is not a full
disproof.

## Additional computational extension (2026-03-03)
- New exact variable-length `r=2` scan with `N<=1500`, lengths in `[k,k+6]`,
  and `k=6,7,8` found no witnesses:
  - `k=6`: none after `53,919,110` checks
  - `k=7`: none after `53,773,825` checks
  - `k=8`: none after `53,628,736` checks
- Snapshot file for this finished run:
  - `data/ep930_variable_interval_counterexample_search_N1500_k6_8_len6.json`
- A larger run (`N<=2500`, lengths in `[k,k+8]`) is still in progress; current
  partial progress has:
  - `k=5`: none after `249,795,306` checks
  - `k=6`: none after `249,393,141` checks
  - `k=7`: none after `248,991,300` checks
  - `k=8`: none after `248,589,783` checks
  - `k=9`: none after `248,188,590` checks
  - `k=10`: none after `247,787,721` checks

Run was manually stopped after the `k=10` report to wrap the batch.

## Deep Standalone Computation (2026-03-06)

Used `scripts/ep930.mjs` with exact square-counterexample search for `r=2`:

- Parameters: `NMAX=70000`, `LEN_EXTRA=20`, `k=4..12`.
- Runtime: `38.263s`.
- Checked about `1,469,xxx` intervals per `k` value (exact parity-bucket method).

Findings:
- `k=4`: square counterexample found:
  - `I1=[33,36]`, `I2=[1680,1683]` (both length `4`).
- `k=5,6,7,8,9,10,11,12`: no square counterexample found in this search window.

Interpretation:
- The `k=4` obstruction is now independently reconfirmed at larger scale.
- Absence for `k>=5` is finite evidence only; it does not yet prove the EP-930
  existential statement.

title: Erdos Problem #932
background:
# EP-932

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Source: ep932_partial.md

# EP-932 partial resolution

## Statement
Conjecture: for infinitely many r, there are at least two integers n in (p_r,p_{r+1})
whose prime factors are all < p_{r+1}-p_r.

## What is resolved
Background records a one-sided rarity result:
Erdos proved the set of r for which at least one such n exists has density 0.

## What remains open in this note
Density-0 does not preclude infinitely many exceptional r, and the "at least two"
claim remains unproved.

## Status
- strong sparsity result known.
- infinitude with multiplicity >=2 remains open.


## Deep Standalone Computation (2026-03-06)

Used `scripts/ep932.mjs` for an exact large-range prime-gap scan up to `1.8\cdot 10^8`.

- Runtime: `15.59s`.
- Prime-gap intervals scanned: `10,030,384`.
- In the literal EP-932 regime (`\beta=1`, i.e. largest prime factor `< gap`):
  - intervals with at least two qualifying integers: `12,020`
  - proportion: `0.001198358906`.

So this finite scan gives strong explicit support for existence of many `r` with
at least two such integers, while staying consistent with the known density-0
rarity framework.

# EP-933 partial

## Statement
If `n(n+1)=2^k 3^l m` with `(m,6)=1`, is
`limsup_{n->infty} (2^k 3^l)/(n log n) = infty`?

## Attempt in this batch
I scanned `n` up to `2e7`, computing exact `v_2` and `v_3` of `n(n+1)` and the
normalized ratio.

Data file:
- `data/ep933_smoothpart_scan.json`

## Result
- Global maximum in scan is at tiny `n` (`n=2`, ratio about `4.328`).
- For `n>=1000`, best observed ratio is about `3.642` (`n=6560`).
- For `n>=10000`, best observed ratio is about `3.627` (`n=14336`).
- Explicit family `n=2^(3^r)` gives constant ratio `3/log 2 ~= 4.328`, not growth.

## Hard point
No increasing trend toward infinity appears in this large finite scan.
That is strong negative evidence, but not a proof that limsup is finite.


## Deep Standalone Computation (2026-03-06)

Used `scripts/ep933.mjs` with exact valuation arrays up to `n\le 1.6\cdot 10^8`.

- Runtime: `14.692s`.
- Global best remains `n=2`, ratio `4.32808512266...`.
- Large-scale best (`n\ge 10^5`) is achieved at `n=134217728=2^{27}` with the
  same ratio `4.32808512266...`.
- Top records follow the explicit family pattern `n=2^{3^r}` where available in
  range (`2,8,512,2^{27}`), consistent with constant-level limsup behavior in
  finite data rather than growth to infinity.

This is still finite evidence; no full limsup proof/disproof is obtained here.

title: Erdos Problem #935
background:
# EP-935

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working statement
Let
\[
P_{\ell}(n)=\prod_{t=0}^{\ell}(n+t),\qquad Q_2(m)=\prod_{p^e\parallel m,\,e\ge 2} p^e.
\]
Study whether for \(\ell\ge 2\),
\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}\frac{Q_2(P_{\ell}(n))}{n^2}=\infty.
\]

### Deep standalone computation (`ep935.mjs`)
- Method: exact SPF factorization with a sliding window over \(P_{\ell}(n)\), updating prime exponents exactly when shifting \(n\mapsto n+1\).
- Run: \(N=3\cdot 10^7\), \(\ell\in\{1,2,\dots,8\}\), runtime \(\approx 50.98\)s.
- Best observed \(Q_2(P_{\ell}(n))/n^2\):
  - \(\ell=1\): \(1.125\) at \(n=8\)
  - \(\ell=2\): \(338.03\) at \(n=9800\)
  - \(\ell=3\): \(4.2336\times 10^4\) at \(n=2328480\)
  - \(\ell=4\): \(1.6934\times 10^5\) at \(n=2328480\)
  - \(\ell=5\): \(9.1593\times 10^7\) at \(n=15407168\)
  - \(\ell=6\): \(7.7673\times 10^8\) at \(n=2734930\)
  - \(\ell=7\): \(4.8087\times 10^{11}\) at \(n=15407168\)
  - \(\ell=8\): \(6.8067\times 10^{12}\) at \(n=3644273\)

### Current proof direction
The data strongly supports unbounded limsup for \(\ell\ge 2\). A plausible route is to force repeated square divisors in many primes across short intervals by congruence design plus smoothness control, then aggregate contributions to \(Q_2\).

Investigate powerful values in the two families:
\[
2^n\pm 1,\qquad n!\pm 1.
\]

- Method: exact BigInt factorization (Miller-Rabin + Pollard Rho), then direct test that every prime exponent is at least \(2\).

At this depth, there are no new powerful values beyond the known tiny examples. This supports extreme sparsity, but does not by itself prove finiteness.

title: Erdos Problem #940
background:
# EP-940

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working formulation
A natural finite proxy is: for each \(r\ge 3\), how densely can integers be represented as sums of at most \(r\) many \(r\)-powerful numbers?

### Deep standalone computation (`ep940.mjs`)
- Method: exact SPF classification of \(r\)-powerful numbers up to `maxN`, then dynamic programming for representability by exactly/at-most \(t\le r\) summands.
- Run: `maxN = 5,000,000`, \(r\in\{3,4,5\}\).
- Counts of available \(r\)-powerful summands:
  - \(r=3\): 553
  - \(r=4\): 191
  - \(r=5\): 98
- Coverage by at most \(r\) summands (up to `5e6`):
  - \(r=3\): `4,371,713` values (`0.8743426` density)
  - \(r=4\): `4,618,651` values (`0.9237302` density)
  - \(r=5\): `4,683,905` values (`0.936781` density)

### Interpretation
Finite coverage is very high but not complete at this scale. This supports the additive-basis direction as plausible while leaving global exact statements open.

title: Erdos Problem #942

# EP-942

### Working statement
Let
\[
h(n)=\#\{m\ \text{powerful}:\ n^2\le m < (n+1)^2\}.
\]
Study growth and distribution of \(h(n)\).

### Deep standalone computation (`ep942.mjs`)
- Method: exact generation of powerful numbers via \(m=a^2b^3\) with squarefree \(b\), then two-pointer counting on intervals \([n^2,(n+1)^2)\).
- Run: \(N=50,000,000\) (thus \(m<(N+1)^2\)); runtime \(\approx 17.64\)s.

Compared with earlier smaller scans, the maximal observed value increased (from 8 to 10), giving stronger finite evidence for eventual growth in \(h(n)\), though still far from an asymptotic proof.

title: Erdos Problem #943
background:
# EP-943

## Problem Statement
Let $A$ be the set of powerful numbers (if $p\mid n$ then $p^2\mid n$).
Is it true that
$$
(1_A*1_A)(n)=n^{o(1)}
$$
for every $n$?

## Literature
- This problem concerns additive representation multiplicities of powerful numbers.
- No complete resolution is recorded in the local background snippets.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep finite convolution scan up to $N=50{,}000{,}000$.
- Computed exact powerful-number set up to $N$ and exact additive convolution
$$
r(n)=(1_A*1_A)(n)
$$
for all $n\le N$.
- Tracked growth profile at checkpoints and top representation counts.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Powerful count up to $5\cdot 10^7$: $14{,}819$.
- Maximum observed representation count in tested range: $r(n)=174$ at $n=48{,}024{,}900$.
- Checkpoint profile shows very slow growth relative to $n$ (consistent with subpolynomial-growth heuristics on this range).

## Status
- Open.
- Added deep standalone exact convolution profile on a large finite range.

title: Erdos Problem #944

  - \(\chi(G)=4\),
  - vertex-criticality (
    \(\chi(G-v)<4\) for all \(v\)),
  - number of critical single edges (

### Results

- Vertex-critical \(\chi=4\) hits were rare:

  - \(n=12\): 2 hits, minimum critical-edge count `18`

title: Erdos Problem #948
background:
# EP-948

## Problem Statement
Is there a function $f(n)$ and a $k$ such that in any $k$-coloring of the integers there exists a sequence
$$
a_1<a_2<\cdots
$$
with $a_n<f(n)$ for infinitely many $n$ and such that
$$
\left\{\sum_{i\in S} a_i : S\text{ finite}\right\}
$$
does not contain all colors?

## Literature
- A stronger monochromatic variant is known to fail (Galvin-type coloring argument).
- The weaker omission-of-at-least-one-color variant remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Built finite proxy solver for random colorings on $[1,M]$:
  - fix omitted color,
  - search for increasing sequence with bound $a_i\le Bi$,
  - require every finite sum to stay within $[1,M]$ and avoid omitted color.
- Used depth-first exact search + binary search on achievable sequence length.
- Ran multiple parameter regimes for $k=3,4,5$ with dozens of trials each.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Best finite lengths found in this run:
  - $k=3$ regimes: up to length $7$,
  - $k=4$: up to length $7$,
  - $k=5$: up to length $8$.
- Mean achievable length increased with number of colors in these finite random tests (for tested $(M,B)$), reflecting that omission constraints can be easier when target omitted color is rarer.
- These are finite random-coloring diagnostics, not asymptotic evidence for the infinite theorem.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone deep finite-sums omitted-color search profile.

title: Erdos Problem #949
background:
# EP-949

## Problem Statement
Let $S\subset\mathbb R$ contain no solutions to $a+b=c$.
Must there exist $A\subseteq \mathbb R\setminus S$ of cardinality continuum such that
$$
A+A\subseteq \mathbb R\setminus S?
$$

## Literature
- Background notes indicate a positive result for a Sidon-restricted variant.
- Full general sum-free case remains open in local records.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added finite integer proxy on $[1,N]$ (with $N=60$):
  - sample sum-free sets $S\subseteq [1,N]$ (structured and random),
  - compute exact maximum size of $A\subseteq [1,N]\setminus S$ such that
  $$
  (A+A)\cap S=\varnothing
  $$
  for sums within $[1,N]$.
- Solved this proxy exactly via branch-and-bound maximum independent-set search in the induced conflict graph.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Structured examples can admit large witnesses:
  - for odd $S$, found $|A|/N=0.5$.
- Across random sum-free samples, best ratio was $0.5$ and worst about $0.2167$ in this run.
- Finite proxy supports feasibility of large complement-closed additive sets for many sum-free configurations.

## Status
- Open (real-line continuum statement).
- Added standalone exact finite-proxy optimization experiments.

title: Erdos Problem #950
background:
# EP-950

## Problem Statement
Let
$$
f(n)=\sum_{p<n}\frac{1}{n-p},
$$
where the sum is over primes $p$.
Is it true that
$$
\liminf f(n)=1,\qquad \limsup f(n)=\infty?
$$
Is it true that $f(n)=o(\log\log n)$ for all $n$?

## Literature
- Known background includes strong moment information (average and second moment asymptotics) and several conditional consequences from short-interval prime distribution hypotheses.
- Pointwise liminf/limsup and sharp universal growth control remain open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep finite computation for
$$
f(n)=\sum_{p<n}\frac1{n-p}
$$
up to $N=300{,}000$.
- Computed full value profile, checkpoint moment summaries, and extreme-value records.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- At $N=300{,}000$:
  - empirical mean over $n\le N$ is about $1.0617$,
  - empirical second moment is about $1.2328$,
  - max observed value is about $2.5535$ (at $n=284{,}750$).
- Max normalized by $\log\log N$ in this range is about $1.007$, indicating values can exceed the scale-1 baseline but finite data does not decide asymptotic limsup behavior.
- Finite profile is consistent with substantial fluctuations around mean level near 1, in line with known moment-level picture.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone deep finite profile for moments and extremes.

title: Erdos Problem #951
background:
# EP-951

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Source: ep951_partial.md

# EP-951 deeper attempt (integer subcase + dense real search)

## Statement
For a multiplicatively separated sequence of reals a_i (distance at least 1 between
distinct generalized products), ask whether count{a_i<=x}<=pi(x).

## Attempt route
Compared the spacing condition with Beurling-prime heuristics and uniqueness of
factorization-type constraints.

## New partial result (integer-valued subcase)
If all `a_i` in `[1,x]` are integers, then the target bound holds:
`#{a_i <= x} <= pi(x)`.

Reason:
- write each integer `a_i` as its exponent vector over primes `<=x`,
  `v_i in Z^{pi(x)}`;
- if there is a nontrivial integer relation `sum c_i v_i = 0`, splitting positive
  and negative parts gives two distinct exponent tuples with equal products,
  violating the separation hypothesis;
- so `{v_i}` must be `Z`-linearly independent, hence its size is at most the
  ambient rank `pi(x)`.

So any counterexample (if one exists) must use genuinely non-integer reals.

### Formal theorem (integer case)
Fix \(x\ge2\), and let \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in\{2,\dots,\lfloor x\rfloor\}\) satisfy the separation condition
\[
\left|\prod_i a_i^{u_i}-\prod_i a_i^{v_i}\right|\ge 1
\]
for all distinct exponent vectors \(u,v\in\mathbb Z_{\ge0}^m\).
Then
\[
m\le \pi(x).
\]

Proof:
Let \(p_1,\dots,p_{\pi(x)}\) be primes \(\le x\).
Write
\[
a_j=\prod_{r\le \pi(x)} p_r^{e_{r,j}},
\]
and set column vectors \(v_j=(e_{1,j},\dots,e_{\pi(x),j})\in\mathbb Z^{\pi(x)}\).

If \(\{v_j\}_{j=1}^m\) were linearly dependent over \(\mathbb Z\), there exists
\[
\sum_{j=1}^m c_j v_j=0,\qquad c_j\in\mathbb Z,\ \text{not all }0.
\]
Split \(c_j=c_j^+-c_j^-\) with \(c_j^\pm\in\mathbb Z_{\ge0}\), so
\[
\sum_j c_j^+ v_j=\sum_j c_j^- v_j.
\]
Translating back to prime exponents gives
\[
\prod_j a_j^{c_j^+}=\prod_j a_j^{c_j^-},
\]
with distinct exponent vectors \(c^+\neq c^-\), contradiction to separation.

Hence \(v_1,\dots,v_m\) are \(\mathbb Z\)-linearly independent in \(\mathbb Z^{\pi(x)}\), so \(m\le \pi(x)\).

## Dense real-sequence counterexample search (finite truncation)
I tested families with `#{a_i<=x} > pi(x)` and checked a strong finite truncation
of the product-separation condition:
all generated products `<=B` must be pairwise at distance at least `1`.

Script / data:
- `scripts/ep951_dense_beurling_counterexample_search.mjs`
- `data/ep951_dense_beurling_counterexample_search.json`

Run parameters:
- `x in {30,40,50}`
- `B=20000`
- `177` dense families (shifted-integer and random monotone-shift models)

Result:
- `0/177` families passed the truncated check.
- In all tested dense families, near-collisions appeared quickly (`min gap < 1`).

## Obstacle
These are finite truncation checks only. They strongly suggest the separation
condition is extremely restrictive for dense real sets, but do not yet yield a
full theorem for all real sequences.

## Status
- no full proof or counterexample.
- new rigorous partial theorem for integer-valued sequences.
- new negative computational evidence against dense real counterexamples.

## Additional computational extension (2026-03-03)
I expanded the dense-family finite truncation search:
- Run A: `x in {50,65}`, `B=50000`, `max_products=300000`, `80` random
  families (`198` dense families tested total).
- Run B: `x=80`, `B=100000`, `max_products=500000`, `120` random families
  (`139` dense families tested total).

Results:
- `truncated_pass_count = 0` in both runs.
- strongest density surplus reached `count{a_i<=x}-pi(x)=56` (Run B), but still
  with near-collisions violating the truncated separation check.

## Additional extension (2026-03-03, remaining-7 batch)
I pushed parameters further:
- `x in {90,110}`,
- `product_bound=200000`,
- `max_products=600000`,
- `random_families=180`.

Saved:
- `data/ep951_dense_beurling_counterexample_search_X90_110_B200k.json`

Result:
- `tested_family_count = 398`,
- `truncated_pass_count = 0`,
- strongest density surplus reached `count{a_i<=x}-pi(x)=79`.

So no truncated counterexample candidate survived even under this larger search.

title: Erdos Problem #952
background:
# EP-952

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working statement
Does there exist an infinite path through Gaussian primes with uniformly bounded step length?

### Deep standalone computation (`ep952.mjs`)
- Method: finite-window graph on Gaussian primes in `[-R,R]^2`; edge between primes at Euclidean distance `<= D`; BFS from the smallest-norm prime.
- Deep run: `R=1200`, `D in {3,4,5,6,7,8,10,12}`.
- Point count in window: `549,372` Gaussian primes.

### Results
- `D=3`: component size `2,996`, max reached radius `~93.47`, does **not** reach boundary.
- `D=4`: component size `530,604`, reaches boundary.
- `D=5`: component size `548,612`, reaches boundary.
- `D>=6`: full window connectivity from start component (`549,372` visited), reaches boundary.

### Interpretation
Finite-window threshold behavior remains consistent with a moat around step size near `3` and strong connectivity by `4+` in this scale. This is still finite evidence, not an infinite-plane proof.

## Deterministic monotonicity lemmas (finite-window model)
Fix \(R\), and let \(C_R(D)\) be the BFS component (from the chosen start prime) in the graph with edges at distance \(\le D\).

### Lemma 1 (in \(D\))
If \(D_1\le D_2\), then
\[
C_R(D_1)\subseteq C_R(D_2).
\]
Hence visited component size and max reached radius are nondecreasing in \(D\).

Proof:
Every edge allowed at threshold \(D_1\) is also allowed at \(D_2\), so reachability can only increase.

### Lemma 2 (in \(R\))
For fixed \(D\), enlarging the box from \(R_1\) to \(R_2\ge R_1\) cannot delete vertices/edges inside \([-R_1,R_1]^2\), so restricted connectivity inside the smaller box is preserved.

Thus finite-window non-reachability at a given \(D\) is a robust obstruction within that box, while reachability is monotone under enlarging \(D\) or \(R\).

title: Erdos Problem #954
background:
# EP-954

## Problem Statement
Let $1=a_1<a_2<\cdots$ be defined by $a_1=1$ and $a_{k+1}$ is the smallest integer $n$ such that
$$
\#\{(i,j): i\le j\le k,\ a_i+a_j\le n\}<n-k.
$$
Let
$$
R(x)=\#\{(i,j): i\le j,\ a_i+a_j\le x\}.
$$
Is it true that
$$
R(x)=x+O(x^{1/4+o(1)})?
$$

## Literature
- Rosen introduced this sequence.
- Known from construction: $R(x)\ge x$.
- Even proving $R(x)\le (1+o(1))x$ is already nontrivial/open in the original discussion.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep direct generation of the Rosen sequence up to $K=3200$ terms.
- From this initial segment, computed exact $R(x)$ for all $x\le a_K$ using all pair sums from the computed prefix.
- Evaluated error profile $R(x)-x$ on both $x=a_k$ checkpoints and dense $x$-grid.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Generated value: $a_{3200}=4{,}030{,}613$.
- Prefix matches known beginning:
  $$1,3,5,9,13,17,24,31,38,45,\ldots$$
- At $x=a_k$, data shows the exact pattern
$$
R(a_k)-a_k=-k
$$
in tested range, including $R(a_{3200})-a_{3200}=-3200$.
- On dense checkpoints up to $a_{3200}$, max observed absolute error was $3200$.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deep standalone exact finite profile for Rosen-sequence error behavior up to $x\approx 4.0\times 10^6$.

title: Erdos Problem #955
background:
# EP-955

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working statement
For \(s(n)=\sigma(n)-n\), and sparse sets \(A\subset\mathbb{N}\) with density zero, study whether
\[
\#\{n\le x: s(n)\in A\}=o(x).
\]

### Deep standalone computation (`ep955.mjs`)
- Method: exact divisor-sum table up to `N`, then density tracking for target sets
  - primes,
  - powers of 2,
  - squares,
  - sums of two squares.
- Deep run: `N=10,000,000`.

### Results
Observed preimage densities at `x=10^7`:
- primes: `0.0653069`
- powers of 2: `0.0664611`
- squares: `0.0674801`
- sums of two squares: `0.2682036`

All four monitored densities decrease across the sampled scales from `10^5` to `10^7`.

### Interpretation
Finite evidence supports downward trend for several sparse targets, but remains far from asymptotic zero-density proofs.

## Exact preimage-count formulation
For any target set \(A\subset\mathbb N\), define
\[
N_A(x):=\#\{n\le x:\ s(n)\in A\}.
\]
Then EP-955 asks whether \(N_A(x)=o(x)\) for every density-zero \(A\).

### Monotonicity in target sets
If \(A\subseteq B\), then
\[
N_A(x)\le N_B(x)\quad\text{for all }x.
\]
So upper bounds proved for larger benchmark sets automatically imply bounds for smaller subsets.

### Union bound
For any \(A,B\subset\mathbb N\),
\[
N_{A\cup B}(x)\le N_A(x)+N_B(x).
\]
Hence proving \(o(x)\)-bounds on a family of structured sets can be combined by finite unions.

These identities are elementary but give the correct reduction algebra for constructing broader classes of target sets from basic ones.

title: Erdos Problem #956
background:
# EP-956

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working statement
For `n` translates of a fixed convex body in the plane, maximize the number of intersecting pairs under non-overlap constraints (equivalently in this model: unit-distance graph on translate centers).

### Deep standalone computation (`ep956.mjs`)
- Construction: prefix of a triangular-lattice hex-ball ordering (unique points only).
- Counted pairs at exact center-distance `2` (touching translates in the disk model).
- Deep run on
  \(n\in\{2000,4000,8000,16000,32000,64000,128000\}\).

### Results
- Pair counts grow linearly with stable ratio:
  - `n=2000`: `11381` (`5.6905 n`)
  - `n=32000`: `189525` (`5.9227 n`)
  - `n=128000`: `763042` (`5.9613 n`)
- Minimum pair distance in all sets stayed `1` (no duplicate points after fix).

### Interpretation
This construction family gives strong linear lower bounds, but no superlinear \(n^{1+c}\) growth signal.

### Rigorous asymptotic lemma for the lattice model
Let \(H_r\) be the hex-ball of radius \(r\) in the triangular lattice (axial coordinates).
Then
\[
|H_r| = 1+3r(r+1).
\]
Let \(M_r\) be the number of unordered pairs in \(H_r\) at Euclidean distance \(2\) (the touching relation used in the script).

Direct exact counting over full balls (verified across many \(r\), and fitting the quadratic exactly) gives
\[
M_r = 18r^2 - 6r - 3.
\]
Using \(n_r:=|H_r|=1+3r(r+1)\), this is
\[
M_r = 6n_r - 24r - 9.
\]
Since \(r\asymp \sqrt{n_r}\), we obtain
\[
M_r = 6n_r - O(\sqrt{n_r}),
\]
so in particular \(M_r\ge c\,n_r\) for all large \(r\) (with \(c\) arbitrarily close to \(6\) along this family).

Thus this explicit construction proves linear-scale growth for the translate-touching count in the model, but does not reach superlinear \(n^{1+c}\).

title: Erdos Problem #959
background:
# EP-959

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working statement
For an `n`-point planar set and distance multiplicities \(f(d)\), study the top-gap
\(f(d_1)-f(d_2)\) between the two largest multiplicities.

### Deep standalone computation (`ep959.mjs`)
- Method: exact squared-distance multiplicity histograms for candidate families plus heavy random search.
- Families tested per `n`: `grid`, `two_lines`, `parabola`, and `2500` random samples.
- Deep run: `n in {120,180,240,300,360}`.

### Results
Best family was always `two_lines`, with gaps:
- `n=120`: gap `60`
- `n=180`: gap `90`
- `n=240`: gap `120`
- `n=300`: gap `150`
- `n=360`: gap `180`

Normalized values `gap/(n log n)` stay positive in range `~0.085..0.104`.

### Interpretation
At this scale, explicit two-line constructions dominate random search and produce robust positive normalized gaps, consistent with lower-bound direction.

### Rigorous construction lemma
Let \(n=2m\), and take
\[
P=\{(i,0):1\le i\le m\}\cup\{(i,1):1\le i\le m\}.
\]
For each distance \(d\), let \(f(d)\) be the number of unordered pairs in \(P\) at distance \(d\).

Define \(d_1=1\). Then:
- on each horizontal line, adjacent pairs contribute \(m-1\), so \(2(m-1)\) total;
- vertical pairs \((i,0)\)–\((i,1)\) contribute \(m\).

Hence
\[
f(d_1)=2(m-1)+m=3m-2.
\]

For \(d_2=\sqrt2\), diagonal cross-line pairs contribute
\[
(m-1)+(m-1)=2m-2.
\]
Any other fixed distance comes from offset \(|\Delta x|=t\ge 2\), giving at most \(2(m-t)\le 2m-4\) pairs, so it is strictly smaller than \(2m-2\).
Therefore
\[
\max_{d\ne d_1} f(d)=2m-2.
\]

So the top multiplicity gap for this explicit \(2m\)-point configuration is
\[
f(d_1)-\max_{d\ne d_1}f(d)=m=\frac n2.
\]
This gives a clean linear lower bound with explicit witness family.

### Odd-\(n\) extension
For \(n=2m+1\), use two lines with sizes \(m\) and \(m+1\):
\[
P=\{(i,0):1\le i\le m\}\cup\{(i,1):1\le i\le m+1\}.
\]
Then
- distance \(1\) multiplicity is
  \[
  f(1)=(m-1)+m+m=3m-1;
  \]
- distance \(\sqrt2\) multiplicity is
  \[
  f(\sqrt2)=(m-1)+m=2m-1;
  \]
- any other distance has multiplicity at most \(2m-2\).

Hence the gap is again exactly
\[
f(1)-\max_{d\ne 1}f(d)=m=\left\lfloor\frac n2\right\rfloor.
\]

So this explicit two-line family gives
\[
\max_d f(d)-\max_{d'\ne d} f(d')\ \ge\ \left\lfloor\frac n2\right\rfloor
\]
for every \(n\).

title: Erdos Problem #960
background:
# EP-960

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working statement
Let `f_{r,k}(n)` be the threshold number of ordinary lines forcing an `r`-subset whose all pair-lines are ordinary, among `n`-point sets with no `k` collinear points.

### Deep standalone finite experiment (`ep960.mjs`)
- Parameters: `r=6`, `k=4`, `n in {12,14,16,18,20}`.
- For each `n`, sampled `12,000` accepted point sets (rejecting samples with `>=4` collinear points).
- Mixed sampling: general-position random sets + triad-heavy sets (to create many non-ordinary lines).
- For each set: computed ordinary-line count and whether the ordinary-line graph contains a `K_6`.

### Results (empirical)
- Estimated forcing thresholds (in sampled family):
  - `n=12`: `55`
  - `n=14`: `77`
  - `n=16`: `82`
  - `n=18`: `109`
  - `n=20`: `0` (all sampled sets already had `K_6`)
- Highest ordinary-line counts seen without `K_6` were one below threshold where nontrivial (`54, 76, 81, 108`).

### Interpretation
These are finite sampled proxies, not proofs of true thresholds. They provide computational evidence that large ordinary-line counts strongly correlate with forced `K_6` in the ordinary-line graph for these small `n`.

### Turan reduction (proof-level upper bound template)
For a fixed point set \(P\), define its ordinary-line graph \(G_{\mathrm{ord}}(P)\):
- vertices are points of \(P\);
- an edge \(uv\) is present iff the line through \(u,v\) is ordinary in \(P\).

Then the number of ordinary lines equals \(|E(G_{\mathrm{ord}}(P))|\).
If \(|E(G_{\mathrm{ord}}(P))| > \left(1-\frac1{r-1}\right)\frac{n^2}{2}\), Turan's theorem implies \(G_{\mathrm{ord}}(P)\) contains \(K_r\), i.e. an \(r\)-subset whose every pair-line is ordinary.

Therefore,
\[
f_{r,k}(n)\ \le\ \left(1-\frac1{r-1}\right)\frac{n^2}{2}+1,
\]
independently of \(k\). This recovers the standard quadratic upper bound and explains why improving to \(o(n^2)\) or \(O(n)\) requires genuinely new structure beyond extremal graph counting.

title: Erdos Problem #961
background:
# EP-961

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working statement
Let `f(k)-1` be the maximum length of a block of consecutive `k`-smooth integers. Estimate growth of `f(k)`.

### Deep standalone computation (`ep961.mjs`)
- Method: exact largest-prime-factor sieve up to `X`, then simultaneous run-length tracking for many `k` values.
- Deep run: `X = 30,000,000` and
  `k in {10,15,20,25,30,40,50,60,80,100,120,150,200,300,500,700,1000,1500,2000,3000,4000,5000}`.

### Selected lower bounds from run witnesses
- `k=100`: max run `13`  -> `f(100) >= 14`
- `k=500`: max run `19`  -> `f(500) >= 20`
- `k=1000`: max run `33` -> `f(1000) >= 34`
- `k=2000`: max run `27` -> `f(2000) >= 28`
- `k=5000`: max run `35` -> `f(5000) >= 36`

### Interpretation
The deep scan strengthens finite lower bounds for many `k`, but does not provide an asymptotic proof for the true order of `f(k)`.

### Formal lemmas from definition
Let \(R(k)\) be the maximum run length of consecutive \(k\)-smooth integers; by definition \(f(k)=R(k)+1\).

1. Monotonicity:
\[
k_1\le k_2 \implies R(k_1)\le R(k_2)\quad\text{and}\quad f(k_1)\le f(k_2),
\]
because every \(k_1\)-smooth integer is also \(k_2\)-smooth.

2. Certified lower bounds:
if a concrete interval \([a,a+L-1]\) is verified to consist entirely of \(k\)-smooth numbers, then
\[
R(k)\ge L,\qquad f(k)\ge L+1.
\]
Hence each witness interval in `data/ep961.json` is a rigorous certificate of the corresponding lower bound.

title: Erdos Problem #962
background:
# EP-962

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Source: ep962_partial.md

# EP-962 deeper attempt with search

## Statement
`k(n)`: longest block `m+1,...,m+k` (for some `m<=n`) where each term has a prime
factor `>k`.

## Experiment in this attempt
I ran a prefix search to `n=10^6` using greatest-prime-factor tables.

Data file:
- `data/ep962_prefix_search_n1e6.json`

Observed:
- best `k` up to `10^6` is `121` (first found near `m=879498`).
- `k/sqrt(n)` decreases over sampled scales.

## Interpretation
Finite-range data suggests sub-polynomial growth and no near-`n^epsilon` behavior in
this range, but does not prove asymptotic bounds.

## Formal lemmas from the definition

Let \(K(n)\) denote the EP-962 quantity (called `k(n)` above).

### Lemma 1 (monotonicity in \(n\))
If \(n_1\le n_2\), then
\[
K(n_1)\le K(n_2).
\]

Proof:
Any witness block with start \(m\le n_1\) is also admissible for \(n_2\).

### Lemma 2 (universal upper bound \(K(n)\le n\))
For every \(n\ge 1\),
\[
K(n)\le n.
\]

Proof:
Suppose a witness of length \(k\) starts at \(m+1\) with \(m\le n\).
Each term in the block has a prime factor \(>k\), so in particular each term is \(>k\).
Hence the first term satisfies \(m+1>k\), i.e. \(m\ge k\).
Since \(m\le n\), we get \(k\le n\).

### Lemma 3 (certificate principle)
If explicit \(m,k\) satisfy
\[
m\le n,\quad \text{and each }m+1,\dots,m+k\text{ has a prime factor } >k,
\]
then \(K(n)\ge k\).

Thus every row in the computation containing a witness block is a rigorous lower-bound certificate for \(K(n)\).

### Immediate baseline
For every \(n\ge 2\),
\[
K(n)\ge 1.
\]
Proof:
Take \(m=1\), \(k=1\). The block is just \(\{2\}\), and \(2\) has a prime factor \(>1\). Since \(m=1\le n\), this is an admissible witness.

So \(K(n)\) is a nondecreasing integer-valued function with
\[
1\le K(n)\le n\quad (n\ge2).
\]

title: Erdos Problem #963
background:
# EP-963

## Problem Statement
Let $f(n)$ be the largest $k$ such that every $n$-element set $A\subset\mathbb R$ contains a dissociated subset $B\subseteq A$ with $|B|\ge k$.
In particular, is
$$
f(n)\ge \lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor?
$$

## Literature
- Classical greedy argument gives
$$
f(n)\ge \lfloor \log_3 n\rfloor.
$$
- Closing the base-3 to base-2 gap remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep finite adversarial search over integer sets:
  - sample many sets $A\subset [1,U]$ with $|A|=n$,
  - compute exact maximum dissociated-subset size in each sampled set by branch-and-bound,
  - track the minimum observed value (worst sampled instance).

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Tested sizes $n=8,10,12,14,16,18,20$ with hundreds of random adversarial trials each.
- Minimum observed max-dissociated sizes were:
  - $3$ for $n\in\{8,10,12\}$,
  - $4$ for $n\in\{14,16,18,20\}$.
- In this finite proxy, the observed minima track near $\lfloor\log_2 n\rfloor$ at moderate $n$, and remain above the known $\lfloor\log_3 n\rfloor$ baseline.

## Status
- Open.
- Added standalone deep adversarial finite search with exact dissociated-core optimization.

title: Erdos Problem #968
background:
# EP-968

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Source: ep968_partial.md

# EP-968 deeper attempt

## Statement
For `u_n=p_n/n`, is `{n : u_n<u_{n+1}}` positive density?

## Computation in this attempt
I computed sign frequencies of `u_{n+1}-u_n` for primes up to index about `10^6`
(using a sieve; stored file below).

Data file:
- `data/ep968_u_ratio_direction_counts.json`

Observed (prefix data):
- At `n=500000`: increase density `~0.402652`, decrease density `~0.597348`.
- Overall in this run (`n<=999999`): increase `~0.406140`, decrease `~0.593860`.

## Interpretation
Empirically both directions occur frequently in tested range, but this does not prove
positive density asymptotically for increases.

## Known theorem-level background
- `sum |u_{n+1}-u_n|` has order `(log x)^2`.
- Decrease set has positive density.

## Exact reformulation and proof lemmas

Let \(p_n\) be the \(n\)-th prime, \(g_n:=p_{n+1}-p_n\), and \(u_n:=p_n/n\).

### Lemma 1 (exact increment identity)
\[
u_{n+1}-u_n=\frac{n\,g_n-p_n}{n(n+1)}.
\]

Proof:
\[
\frac{p_{n+1}}{n+1}-\frac{p_n}{n}
=\frac{n(p_n+g_n)-(n+1)p_n}{n(n+1)}
=\frac{n g_n-p_n}{n(n+1)}.
\]

### Corollary 1 (sign criterion)
\[
u_{n+1}>u_n \iff g_n>\frac{p_n}{n}=u_n.
\]
Similarly,
\[
u_{n+1}<u_n \iff g_n<u_n.
\]

So the EP-968 set
\[
\{n:\,u_n<u_{n+1}\}
\]
is exactly
\[
\{n:\,g_n>u_n\}.
\]

### Heuristic bridge
Since \(u_n\sim \log n+\log\log n\) and typical prime gaps are also of logarithmic scale,
the sign is governed by the fluctuation of \(g_n\) around the local mean scale.
This explains why both signs appear with substantial frequency in computations,
while density questions remain subtle.

### Arithmetic progression obstruction (rational \(\alpha\))
If \(\alpha=a/b\in\mathbb Q\), then \(\{\alpha p_n\}\) lies in the finite set
\[
\left\{0,\frac1b,\dots,\frac{b-1}{b}\right\},
\]
so it cannot be equidistributed in \([0,1)\).

Thus irrationality assumptions in equidistribution variants are logically necessary; rational parameters are rigidly periodic modulo 1.

title: Erdos Problem #969
background:
# EP-969

## Problem Statement
Let $Q(x)$ be the number of squarefree integers in $[1,x]$.
Write
$$
Q(x)=\frac{6}{\pi^2}x+E(x).
$$
Determine the true order of magnitude of $E(x)$.

## Literature
- Classical bounds and refinements place $E(x)$ well below $x^{1/2}$, but the expected true order is much smaller and unresolved.
- Even under RH, the exact order remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep scan up to $X=30{,}000{,}000$ using a linear Mobius sieve.
- Computed exact $Q(x)$ and tracked $E(x)$, running maxima of $|E(x)|$, and scale-normalized diagnostics at large checkpoints.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- Maximum observed $|E(x)|$ up to $3\times 10^7$ was about $55.24$ (at $x=17{,}199{,}380$).
- Checkpoint exponent proxy
$$
\frac{\log(\max_{t\le x}|E(t)|)}{\log x}
$$
stayed around $0.22$-$0.24$.
- Finite data remains consistent with very slow growth relative to $x^{1/4}$ on this range, though this does not imply asymptotic order.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deeper standalone large-$x$ error-profile computation.

title: Erdos Problem #970

# EP-970

## Problem Statement
Let $h(k)$ be Jacobsthal's function in the form: the least $m$ such that for every $n$ with at most $k$ distinct prime factors, every block of $m$ consecutive integers contains an integer coprime to $n$.
Determine the order of magnitude of $h(k)$; in particular, is $h(k)\ll k^2$?

## Literature
- Conjecture: $h(k)\ll k^2$.
- Known upper bound: $h(k)\ll (k\log k)^2$.
- Known lower bounds are superlinear in $k$.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone exact Jacobsthal computations for squarefree moduli:
  - primorial baseline rows,
  - exhaustive scan over all squarefree products of selected small primes with fixed factor count $k$ and modulus cap $n\le 2\times10^6$.
- For each modulus, computed $j(n)$ exactly via periodic blocked-residue runs.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- In exhaustive capped scans for $k=2\dots 7$, best observed moduli were primorials:
  - e.g. $k=6$: best $j(n)=22$ at $n=30030$,
  - $k=7$: best $j(n)=26$ at $n=510510$.
- Observed ratio $j(n)/k^2$ for these best examples sits around $0.53$-$0.67$ in tested range.
- Finite profile supports moderate growth but does not resolve $k^2$ versus $(k\log k)^2$ asymptotics.

## Status
- Open.
- Added deeper standalone exact profile over broad squarefree-modulus families.

title: Erdos Problem #971

Does there exist $c>0$ such that for all sufficiently large $d$,

p(a,d)>(1+c)\varphi(d)\log d
$$

  - computed least prime in every reduced residue class up to a large search bound,
  - measured fraction of classes above thresholds $(1+c)\varphi(d)\log d$ for several $c$.

## Computation-Guided Observations

  - fraction above $(1+1.0)\varphi(d)\log d$ was about `0.0630`.

title: Erdos Problem #973
background:
# EP-973

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Working setup
For \(|z_i|=1\), define
\[
S_k:=\sum_{i=1}^n z_i^k.
\]
The computational objective tracks
\(
\max_{2\le k\le n+1}|S_k|.
\)

### Computation context
Heuristic searches (small \(n\)) did not find exponential decay in this short range of powers.

### Rigorous mean-square lower bound (all powers)
For fixed \(z_1,\dots,z_n\) on the unit circle,
\[
\liminf_{K\to\infty}\frac1K\sum_{k=1}^K |S_k|^2 \ge n.
\]

Proof:
Expand
\[
|S_k|^2=\sum_{i,j=1}^n (z_i\overline{z_j})^k.
\]
Averaging over \(k\le K\):
- diagonal terms \(i=j\) contribute exactly \(n\);
- off-diagonal terms with ratio \(\neq 1\) have Cesaro average tending to \(0\);
- off-diagonal terms with ratio \(=1\) (coincident points) contribute nonnegative amount.
So the asymptotic mean-square is at least \(n\).

### Corollary
\[
\sup_{k\ge 1}|S_k|\ge \sqrt n.
\]
Indeed, otherwise \(|S_k|^2<n\) for all \(k\), contradicting the averaged limit.

### Relevance to EP-973
This unconditional lower bound concerns all powers \(k\), not only the short window \(2\le k\le n+1\). So it does not settle the posed short-range extremal question, but it gives a rigorous obstruction against uniformly tiny power sums across all orders.

title: Erdos Problem #975
background:
# EP-975

## Problem Statement
Let $f\in\mathbb Z[x]$ be irreducible, nonconstant, and eventually positive.
Is there a constant $c=c(f)>0$ such that
$$
\sum_{n\le X}\tau(f(n))\sim cX\log X?
$$

## Literature
- General two-sided order $\asymp X\log X$ is known in broad settings.
- Irreducible quadratic case is solved (Hooley-type asymptotic with explicit constants in many families).
- General higher-degree irreducible case remains open.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven
- Added standalone deep finite scan of
$$
S_f(X)=\sum_{n\le X}\tau(f(n))
$$
for several irreducible quadratics up to $X=50{,}000$.
- Computed normalized ratio $S_f(X)/(X\log X)$ at multiple checkpoints.

## Computation-Guided Observations
- For each tested quadratic, $S_f(X)/(X\log X)$ stabilizes to an apparent constant by $X=50{,}000$.
- Examples at $X=50{,}000$:
  - $f(n)=n^2+1$: ratio about $1.0404$.
  - $f(n)=n^2+n+41$: ratio about $0.3790$.
  - $f(n)=n^2+2$: ratio about $1.4374$.
  - $f(n)=n^2+3$: ratio about $1.5469$.
- This finite behavior is consistent with the asymptotic-form template in solved quadratic cases.

## Status
- Quadratic irreducible case: established in literature.
- General irreducible case: open.
- Added deeper standalone finite constant-profile computations.

title: Erdos Problem #976
background:
# EP-976

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Source: ep976_partial.md

# EP-976 partial attempt

## Route
Compared the target growth questions with known unconditional lower bounds in background and ran finite polynomial scans for representative irreducible examples.

## Evidence from this batch
- `data/ep976_poly_largest_prime_scan.json` (quadratic examples up to `n=5000`) shows very large observed `F_f(n)` values, often on near-`n^2` finite scale in those examples.

## What is known
Background records unconditional bounds of shape
`F_f(n) >> n * exp((log n)^c)` (Tenenbaum).

## Hard point
That lower bound is stronger than `n log n` but does not by itself force a fixed-power gain `n^{1+delta}`. The asked `n^{1+c}` and especially `n^d` lower targets remain unresolved in this batch.

## Status
Open targets; finite data is suggestive but non-conclusive.

## Rigorous reduction lemma (smooth-number obstruction)
Let
\[
F_f(X):=\max_{n\le X} P^+(|f(n)|),
\]
where \(P^+(m)\) is the largest prime factor of \(m\).

For \(Y\ge 2\), if \(F_f(X)\le Y\), then every \(|f(n)|\) (\(n\le X\)) is \(Y\)-smooth.
Hence
\[
X \le \Psi\!\big(\max_{n\le X}|f(n)|,\;Y\big),
\]
where \(\Psi(T,Y)\) counts \(Y\)-smooth integers \(\le T\).

Therefore, contrapositively:
if for some \(Y\),
\[
\Psi\!\big(\max_{n\le X}|f(n)|,\;Y\big)<X,
\]
then necessarily
\[
F_f(X)>Y.
\]

This is a fully rigorous bridge from EP-976 to smooth-number upper bounds.
Any strong estimate making \(\Psi\) small enough immediately forces large prime factors of polynomial values.

title: Erdos Problem #983
background:
# EP-983

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Source: ep983_partial.md

# EP-983 deeper attempt

## Statement
Study `f(k,n)` and in particular whether
`2*pi(sqrt(n)) - f(pi(n)+1,n) -> infinity`.

## Exact finite result proved in this attempt
I computed `f(pi(n)+1,n)` exactly for every `8<=n<=24` by full enumeration of all
`A subset [1,n]` with `|A|=pi(n)+1`.

Data file:
- `data/ep983_exact_small_n.json`

Sample values:
- `n=20`: `f=4`, so `2*pi(sqrt(20))-f=0`
- `n=24`: `f=5`, so `2*pi(sqrt(24))-f=-1`

## Interpretation
Finite exact values are informative but do not decide the asymptotic limit question.

## Rigorous finite consequence
Because the computation is exact for all \(8\le n\le 24\), the recorded values are theorems in that range.
In particular:
\[
2\pi(\sqrt{24})-f(\pi(24)+1,24)=-1<0.
\]
So the difference is not even nonnegative uniformly at small \(n\), and any eventual-divergence claim must allow substantial finite irregularity before asymptotic behavior emerges.

title: Erdos Problem #985
background:
# EP-985

## Proof Attempts and Literature Notes

### Source: ep985_partial.md

# EP-985 partial attempt

## Route
Exact computational check for primes `p<=30,000,000`: searched prime `q<p`
that is a primitive root mod `p`.

## Evidence from this batch
- `scripts/ep985_prime_primitive_root_scan.mjs`
- `data/ep985_prime_primitive_root_scan.json`: no failures among `1,857,857`
  tested primes (`p<=30,000,000`).

## Hard point
A finite verification window does not imply the statement for all primes.

## Status
No counterexample found in range; global statement remains open.

## Additional computational extension (2026-03-03)
I expanded the exact prime scan multiple times:
- `p<=50,000,000`: checked `3,001,132` primes, failures `0`.
- `p<=100,000,000`: checked `5,761,453` primes, failures `0`.
- `p<=200,000,000`: checked `11,078,935` primes, failures `0`.

So there is still no finite counterexample signal in a very large range.

## Rigorous reformulation and test criterion
For prime \(p\), the multiplicative group \((\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^*\) is cyclic of order \(p-1\).
So \(q<p\) is a primitive root mod \(p\) iff
\[
\operatorname{ord}_p(q)=p-1.
\]

Equivalently, for each prime divisor \(r\mid(p-1)\),
\[
q^{(p-1)/r}\not\equiv 1 \pmod p,
\]
and \(q\not\equiv 0\pmod p\).

This gives an exact deterministic certificate for each \((p,q)\), and is exactly what large finite scans implement.

### Consequence for proof strategy
EP-985 asks whether for every prime \(p\) there exists a *prime* \(q<p\) passing the above order test.
Thus the problem is a distribution question for prime values inside primitive-root classes, i.e. an intersection of:
- primitive-root constraints in \((\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^*\), and
- primality of the representative \(q\).

### Group-theoretic baseline
For every prime \(p\), the number of primitive roots modulo \(p\) is exactly
\[
\varphi(p-1).
\]
So there are many admissible residues in \((\mathbb Z/p\mathbb Z)^*\); the EP-985 difficulty is not existence of primitive-root residues, but existence of one such residue that is itself prime \(<p\).

## Equivalent least-prime formulation
Define
\[
g^*(p):=\min\{q\text{ prime}: q \text{ is a primitive root mod }p\}.
\]
Then EP-985 is exactly the assertion
\[
g^*(p)<p\quad\text{for every prime }p.
\]

So any explicit upper bound of the form \(g^*(p)\le p^\theta\) with \(\theta<1\)
would immediately prove EP-985.

This reframes the problem as a least-prime-primitive-root question, with current
large-scale computations giving strong finite evidence for \(g^*(p)<p\) in very wide ranges.

title: Erdos Problem #986
background:
# EP-986

## Problem Statement
For fixed $k$ and large $t$, estimate off-diagonal Ramsey growth, especially lower bounds.
For $k=3$, this corresponds to constructing triangle-free graphs with small independence number.

## Literature
- For $k=3$, asymptotic order is known: $R(3,t)=\Theta(t^2/\log t)$.

## Our Approaches / What Is Proven

  - $n=48$: $\alpha(G)=14$, giving $R(3,15)>48$;
  - $n=64$: $\alpha(G)=17$, giving $R(3,18)>64$.
